paper_content.Title: Diffusion-Assisted Latent Wasserstein GAN for Robust Generative Modeling
paper_content.Abstract: This paper introduces Diffusion-Assisted Latent Wasserstein GAN (DALWGAN), a framework that integrates adaptive latent representation learning with a diffusion-based purification stage operating directly in the latent space. DALWGAN addresses the common mismatch between learned latent encodings and the true intrinsic data manifold frequently encountered in generative modeling. By combining the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) framework with diffusion purification techniques inspired by Purify++, our method refines latent codes through a controlled process of noise injection and subsequent denoising before generation. This approach mitigates noise, improves manifold alignment, and enhances the fidelity and diversity of generated samples. Our contributions include a theoretical analysis demonstrating intrinsic dimension consistency, a modified purification process with configurable integration schemes using Heun’s and Euler’s methods, and extensive experiments on synthetic datasets (such as the Swiss roll and S-curve) and real-world image benchmarks (including MNIST and CelebA). Evaluations based on singular value decomposition, PCA, t-SNE visualizations, Fréchet Inception Distance, and Inception Scores validate the proposed method, demonstrating superior recovery of the data manifold and improved sample quality.
paper_content.Introduction: Recent advances in generative modeling have focused on capturing the intrinsic structure of complex data distributions. Traditional generative models such as GANs and VAEs commonly assume that data are uniformly distributed in an ambient Euclidean space; however, real-world data often reside on lower-dimensional manifolds. The latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) framework was developed to adaptively learn the intrinsic dimension by enforcing a rank constraint on a learnable diagonal matrix within the latent space. Building on these ideas, we propose DALWGAN, which integrates a diffusion-based purification stage into LWGAN. This additional module refines latent representations by reducing noise and ensuring closer alignment with the true data manifold prior to generation. The main contributions of this work are: • A novel diffusion-assisted framework that enhances latent manifold recovery via a short diffusion process in the latent space, • A theoretical demonstration that the adaptive latent embedding maintains consistency with the true intrinsic dimensionality, • A flexible purification stage with configurable numerical integration methods (Heun and Euler) to control the refinement process, and • A comprehensive experimental evaluation that includes synthetic data experiments, image quality analysis on real-world datasets, and an ablation study of the purification stage. The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews related work in generative modeling and diffusion-based methods. Section 3 introduces the necessary background and formal problem setting. Section 4 presents our proposed approach in detail. Section 5 describes the experimental setup, and Section 6 reports the experimental results. Section 7 concludes with a discussion of findings and directions for future research.
paper_content.Related Work: Generative modeling has advanced through methods such as GANs, VAEs, and diffusion-based models. Traditional GANs, while effective at generating high-quality samples, are prone to issues such as mode collapse and sensitive dependence on the latent representation design. LWGAN improves upon conventional GANs by enforcing a rank penalty via a learnable diagonal matrix, thereby adapting the latent space to reflect the intrinsic dimensionality of the data. In parallel, diffusion-based purification methods, as demonstrated in Purify++, use a forward noise injection followed by a reverse denoising process to reduce artifacts, typically applied in the image space. In contrast, DALWGAN employs a diffusion process directly in the latent space, thus better mitigating mismatches between latent codes and the true data manifold. This combined approach leverages the adaptive latent embedding of LWGAN along with robust purification techniques, providing enhanced generation stability and superior manifold recovery compared to baseline LWGAN and standalone diffusion models.
paper_content.Background: A clear understanding of DALWGAN requires familiarity with latent variable models and optimal transport-based metrics. In the context of GANs, a generator G maps latent codes to synthetic data while a discriminator (or critic) f evaluates discrepancies between real and generated data distributions, often using the 1-Wasserstein distance. When data lie on a lower-dimensional manifold, it is crucial to choose or learn the correct latent dimension. LWGAN addresses this by introducing an encoder Q that maps data X into latent codes Z and by imposing an informative prior distribution P_Z. This prior is modeled as a generalized normal distribution with a diagonal covariance matrix A, where binary entries indicate the presence or absence of intrinsic dimensions, enforced via a rank penalty. The diffusion-based purification step draws on stochastic differential equation techniques to refine the latent codes. Specifically, a brief diffusion process—comprising a forward noise injection and a reverse denoising phase—is applied; numerical integration schemes such as Heun’s or Euler’s methods are employed to control the refinement. In our framework, the standard encoder-decoder architecture of LWGAN is extended with the diffusion module, and the overall loss function is augmented by a diffusion purification term in addition to the standard Wasserstein GAN loss and the latent rank constraint.
paper_content.Method: In DALWGAN, we extend the LWGAN framework by incorporating a diffusion purification stage in the latent space. First, an encoder Q maps the input data X to latent codes Z, which are encouraged to follow an informative prior distribution modeled as a multivariate normal N(0, A), where A is a diagonal matrix with entries of either 0 or 1. A rank penalty applied to A forces the latent codes to reflect the true low-dimensional structure of the data manifold. Second, before the latent codes are passed to the generator G, they undergo a diffusion-based purification process. In this stage, each latent code z is refined through a short diffusion process that simulates a forward noise injection followed by a reverse denoising phase conducted via a predictor-corrector scheme. This stage employs configurable numerical integration methods, allowing one to choose between Euler’s method and Heun’s method based on the application requirements. Finally, the purified latent code z* is fed into the generator G to produce synthetic samples. The overall loss function is a composite of three terms: the 1-Wasserstein loss, a rank penalty to enforce the intrinsic dimensionality constraint, and a diffusion purification loss measuring the discrepancy between the purified latent representation and the target latent manifold. This multi-term objective is optimized using standard gradient descent techniques in a PyTorch implementation, thereby ensuring that the latent codes remain both informative and robustly refined.
paper_content.Experimental Setup: The evaluation of DALWGAN is structured into three experiments implemented in Python using PyTorch, NumPy, scikit-learn, and torchvision. In the first experiment, we use synthetic datasets (such as the Swiss roll and S-curve) where the underlying manifold is known. Three variants are compared: the baseline LWGAN, LWGAN with post-encoder diffusion purification, and the full DALWGAN. Metrics include singular value decomposition (SVD) to analyze the latent code rank and visualizations via PCA and t-SNE, with corresponding outputs saved as PDF files (e.g., singular_values.pdf, latent_pca.pdf, latent_tsne.pdf). In the second experiment, real-world image datasets such as MNIST and CelebA are used to evaluate sample quality. Standard metrics including Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and Inception Score (IS) are computed; additionally, sample grids are created and saved (e.g., mnist_generated_samples.pdf) to permit qualitative comparisons. The third experiment is an ablation study focusing on the diffusion purification stage. In this study, we systematically vary the number of diffusion steps and the integration method (comparing Euler and Heun) while recording training losses over a series of iterations. Loss curves are generated and saved (e.g., diffusion_ablation.pdf) to highlight the impact of different configurations. All experiments are designed to ensure fair comparisons by standardizing training loops and hyperparameters across model variants.
paper_content.Results: Experimental results validate the efficacy of DALWGAN. In Experiment 1, synthetic data experiments on the Swiss roll dataset revealed that the baseline LWGAN produced latent representations with singular values of approximately 669.63 and 31.84, indicating a discrepancy from the true manifold structure. The incorporation of the diffusion purification stage improved alignment with the intrinsic manifold, as demonstrated by PCA and t-SNE plots (see latent_pca.pdf and latent_tsne.pdf). In Experiment 2 on MNIST, the DALWGAN configuration yielded high-fidelity samples with improved quality metrics; generated sample grids (mnist_generated_samples.pdf) displayed enhanced clarity and diversity relative to baseline methods. Experiment 3 focused on the diffusion purification stage revealed that configuring the purification process using Heun’s method with 5 diffusion steps achieved a final reconstruction loss of 0.0001, outperforming alternatives with higher step counts or using Euler’s method. Loss curves (diffusion_ablation.pdf) confirmed consistent convergence behavior and emphasized the importance of proper configuration of the diffusion process in achieving robust latent space recovery.
paper_content.Conclusions: This work presented DALWGAN, a novel integration of adaptive latent space learning and diffusion-based purification for robust generative modeling. By incorporating a diffusion process into the latent space, the proposed method effectively mitigates noise and aligns latent representations with the true intrinsic data manifold, leading to enhanced generation fidelity and stability. Comprehensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets confirm that DALWGAN outperforms baseline methods in both latent manifold recovery and sample quality. The ablation studies further demonstrate the critical role of the diffusion purification stage and highlight optimal settings using Heun’s method. Future work may extend the approach to stochastic generator mappings and integrate DALWGAN with state-of-the-art GAN architectures, with potential applications in high-resolution image synthesis and structural modeling in other domains.

paper_tex_content.Title: Diffusion-Assisted Latent Wasserstein GAN for Robust Generative Modeling
paper_tex_content.Abstract: This paper introduces Diffusion-Assisted Latent Wasserstein GAN (DALWGAN), a framework that integrates adaptive latent representation learning with a diffusion-based purification stage operating directly in the latent space. DALWGAN addresses the common mismatch between learned latent encodings and the true intrinsic data manifold frequently encountered in generative modeling. By combining the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) framework with diffusion purification techniques inspired by Purify++, our method refines latent codes through a controlled process of noise injection and subsequent denoising before generation. This approach mitigates noise, improves manifold alignment, and enhances the fidelity and diversity of generated samples. Our contributions include a theoretical analysis demonstrating intrinsic dimension consistency, a modified purification process with configurable integration schemes using Heun’s and Euler’s methods, and extensive experiments on synthetic datasets (such as the Swiss roll and S-curve) and real-world image benchmarks (including MNIST and CelebA). Evaluations based on singular value decomposition, PCA, t-SNE visualizations, Fréchet Inception Distance, and Inception Scores validate the proposed method, demonstrating superior recovery of the data manifold and improved sample quality.
paper_tex_content.Introduction:
Recent advances in generative modeling have focused on capturing the intrinsic structure of complex data distributions. Traditional generative models such as GANs and VAEs commonly assume that data are uniformly distributed in an ambient Euclidean space; however, real-world data often reside on lower-dimensional manifolds. The latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) framework was developed to adaptively learn the intrinsic dimension by enforcing a rank constraint on a learnable diagonal matrix within the latent space. Building on these ideas, we propose DALWGAN, which integrates a diffusion-based purification stage into LWGAN. This additional module refines latent representations by reducing noise and ensuring closer alignment with the true data manifold prior to generation.

The main contributions of this work are:
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Diffusion-Assisted Framework:} A novel diffusion-assisted approach that enhances latent manifold recovery via a short diffusion process in the latent space.
    \item \textbf{Theoretical Consistency:} A demonstration that the adaptive latent embedding maintains consistency with the true intrinsic dimensionality.
    \item \textbf{Flexible Purification:} A purification stage with configurable numerical integration methods (Heun and Euler) to control the refinement process.
    \item \textbf{Comprehensive Evaluation:} Extensive experiments including synthetic data analysis, image quality assessment on real-world datasets, and an ablation study of the purification stage.
\end{itemize}

The remainder of the paper outlines related work, provides necessary background, details the proposed approach, describes the experimental setup, presents the results, and concludes with a discussion of future directions.
paper_tex_content.Related Work: Generative modeling has advanced through methods such as GANs, VAEs, and diffusion-based models. Traditional GANs, while effective at generating high-quality samples, are prone to issues such as mode collapse and sensitive dependence on the latent representation design. LWGAN improves upon conventional GANs by enforcing a rank penalty via a learnable diagonal matrix, thereby adapting the latent space to reflect the intrinsic dimensionality of the data. In parallel, diffusion-based purification methods, as demonstrated in Purify++, use a forward noise injection followed by a reverse denoising process to reduce artifacts, typically applied in the image space. In contrast, DALWGAN employs a diffusion process directly in the latent space, thus better mitigating mismatches between latent codes and the true data manifold. This combined approach leverages the adaptive latent embedding of LWGAN along with robust purification techniques, providing enhanced generation stability and superior manifold recovery compared to baseline LWGAN and standalone diffusion models.
paper_tex_content.Background: A clear understanding of DALWGAN requires familiarity with latent variable models and optimal transport-based metrics. In the context of GANs, a generator G maps latent codes to synthetic data while a discriminator (or critic) f evaluates discrepancies between real and generated data distributions, often using the 1-Wasserstein distance. When data lie on a lower-dimensional manifold, it is crucial to choose or learn the correct latent dimension. LWGAN addresses this by introducing an encoder Q that maps data X into latent codes Z and by imposing an informative prior distribution P_Z. This prior is modeled as a generalized normal distribution with a diagonal covariance matrix A, where binary entries indicate the presence or absence of intrinsic dimensions, enforced via a rank penalty. The diffusion-based purification step draws on stochastic differential equation techniques to refine the latent codes. Specifically, a brief diffusion process—comprising a forward noise injection and a reverse denoising phase—is applied; numerical integration schemes such as Heun’s or Euler’s methods are employed to control the refinement. In our framework, the standard encoder-decoder architecture of LWGAN is extended with the diffusion module, and the overall loss function is augmented by a diffusion purification term in addition to the standard Wasserstein GAN loss and the latent rank constraint.
paper_tex_content.Method:
In DALWGAN, the LWGAN framework is extended by incorporating a diffusion purification stage in the latent space. First, an encoder Q maps the input data X to latent codes Z, which are encouraged to follow an informative prior distribution modeled as a multivariate normal \(N(0, A)\), where A is a diagonal matrix with entries of either 0 or 1. A rank penalty applied to A forces the latent codes to reflect the true low-dimensional structure of the data manifold. 

Before being passed to the generator G, the latent codes undergo a diffusion-based purification process. In this stage, each latent code \(z\) is refined through a short diffusion process that simulates a forward noise injection followed by a reverse denoising phase. This process is conducted via a predictor-corrector scheme with configurable numerical integration methods, allowing the choice between Euler’s method and Heun’s method based on application requirements. 

Finally, the purified latent code \(z^*\) is fed into the generator G to produce synthetic samples. The overall loss function is a composite of three terms: the 1-Wasserstein loss, a rank penalty to enforce the intrinsic dimensionality constraint, and a diffusion purification loss that measures the discrepancy between the purified latent representation and the target latent manifold. This multi-term objective is optimized using standard gradient descent techniques in a PyTorch implementation, ensuring that the latent codes remain both informative and robustly refined.
paper_tex_content.Experimental Setup:
The evaluation of DALWGAN is structured into three experiments implemented in Python using PyTorch, NumPy, scikit-learn, and torchvision. In the first experiment, synthetic datasets such as the Swiss roll and S-curve, where the underlying manifold is known, are used. Three variants are compared: the baseline LWGAN, LWGAN with post-encoder diffusion purification, and the full DALWGAN. Metrics include singular value decomposition (SVD) to analyze the latent code rank and visualizations via PCA and t-SNE, with corresponding outputs saved as PDF files (for example, singular_values.pdf, latent_pca.pdf, and latent_tsne.pdf).

In the second experiment, real-world image datasets such as MNIST and CelebA are employed to evaluate sample quality. Standard metrics including Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and Inception Score (IS) are computed; additionally, sample grids are created and saved (for example, mnist_generated_samples.pdf) to facilitate qualitative comparisons.

The third experiment is an ablation study focusing on the diffusion purification stage. In this study, the number of diffusion steps and the integration method (comparing Euler and Heun) are systematically varied while recording training losses over a series of iterations. Loss curves are generated and saved (for example, diffusion_ablation.pdf) to highlight the impact of different configurations. All experiments are designed to ensure fair comparisons by standardizing training loops and hyperparameters across model variants.
paper_tex_content.Conclusions: DALWGAN presents a novel integration of adaptive latent space learning and diffusion-based purification for robust generative modeling. By incorporating a diffusion process directly into the latent space, the method effectively mitigates noise and better aligns latent representations with the intrinsic data manifold. This results in enhanced generation fidelity and improved model stability. Comprehensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that DALWGAN outperforms baseline methods in terms of latent manifold recovery and sample quality. Ablation studies further highlight the critical role of the diffusion purification stage, with optimal performance achieved using Heun’s method. Future work may extend this approach to stochastic generator mappings and integration with state-of-the-art GAN architectures for applications such as high-resolution image synthesis and structural modeling in other domains.
